problem_summary: 2015 MCM/ICM Problem C (interdisciplinary): model an organization's "human capital" — a 370-position firm where only ~85% of seats are filled, mid-level positions churn heavily (18%/yr baseline) and churn "diffuses" from departing employees to colleagues. The HR office wants a model that (a) captures the dynamic processes of churn/promotion/recruitment and their direct/indirect effects on productivity and cost, (b) predicts what happens under changed churn rates, and (c) extends the single human-capital network into a MULTILAYER network coupling it to information-flow/trust/friendship/influence layers. This team's answer: a discrete-time, agent/position-based network simulation driven by three coupled evolution algorithms, plus a 3-layer (human-capital + friendship + competition) multilayer-network analysis on a 14-person sub-office.
models_used: Position-as-node static network: 370 positions = nodes, two relationship types as edges — a DIRECTED, UNWEIGHTED affiliation (leader/led) network coded {+1,-1,0} in a 370x370 matrix, and an UNDIRECTED, WEIGHTED cooperation network within divisions (complete subgraphs per team)

Post Archives Matrix (PAM): a 370-row attribute matrix (the team's signature data structure) storing per-position level, division, salary, training/recruitment cost, churn rate, change-count, on-job time, working enthusiasm (0-1), basic productivity, on-job/departure status; travels with the employee on promotion and re-initializes the vacated post

Relationship-compactness / team-performance quantification: affiliation intensity alpha_ij = (d_max - d_ij_min)/d_max from shortest-path distance in the affiliation graph; cooperation intensity delta_team = sum_i sum_j alpha_ij * beta_ij (beta = friendship intensity)

Corporate-performance model as a vector/matrix form: phi = a-vector . (b-vector + mu * c-vector), where a = efficiency/enthusiasm row vector, b = basic productivity, c = on-job time, mu = experience-growth coefficient

Three coupled evolution-driving algorithms over a monthly time step: (1) DISMISSION — low-level can leave immediately, mid/high-level must wait for a backfill, departure raises neighbors' churn rate and lowers their enthusiasm; (2) PROMOTION — fill vacancies top-down by level, internal promotion before recruitment, no grade-skipping, promote highest-tenure employee meeting the next-level productivity threshold; (3) RECRUITMENT — recruit ~2/3 of vacancies (negative-feedback sizing), an on-position-time vector encodes per-level hiring-delay (lag) effects
Monte Carlo simulation: per-employee monthly Bernoulli churn draw calibrated to the annual churn rate; 10 runs averaged to smooth stochastic noise for cost/performance estimates
Training feedback equations: efficiency recovery tau' = tau + xi1*(1-tau) and churn-rate decay nu' = nu - xi2*(nu - 18%), modeling training raising enthusiasm and suppressing turnover

Multilayer network model M=(V_M, E_M, V, L) with 3 layers (human-capital L1, friendship L2, competition L3) over 14 nodes/42 supra-nodes; intra-layer solid edges, inter-layer dotted edges coupling the same person across layers; formalized via adjacency tensor A_ij_Lm_Ln and a supra-adjacency matrix obtained by tensor flattening

Multilayer graph metrics: average degree, clustering coefficient, average path length, graph density computed per layer and for the aggregated multilayer graph (Table 1) to argue the multilayer view carries strictly more information
key_techniques: Reframing 'people' as POSITIONS-as-nodes (370 fixed posts) rather than persons — lets the network topology stay fixed while attributes evolve, neatly matching the '85% filled, mid-level must stay filled' constraints

Superposition trick borrowed from circuit theory: decompose the messy real relationship graph into two independent abstracted networks (affiliation + cooperation) and recombine, justifying treating them separately

Encoding qualitative HR phenomena as quantitative state updates: 'churn diffuses to colleagues' becomes a neighbor enthusiasm-down + churn-rate-up rule; 'loyalty/experience grows productivity' becomes the mu*on-job-time term; 'training helps' becomes the xi1/xi2 recovery equations

Priority-ordered coupling of the three algorithms (churn -> promotion -> recruitment) with a hard invariant that mid/high-level posts never go vacant — a clean state-machine that maps directly onto the problem's stated rules
Recruitment delay modeled as a countdown 'on-position time vector' (decrement each month until 0 = filled), capturing per-level hiring lag without extra machinery
Negative-feedback recruitment sizing (recruit 2/3 of current vacancies) so hiring auto-scales with churn
Monte Carlo + multi-run averaging (10 runs) and an explicit stochastic-error metric w = ((a1+a2+a3)-(a4+a5+a6))/3 to bound run-to-run noise (<0.015)

Control-variate experimental design for the no-external-recruitment scenario: hold all parameters fixed, flip only the mid-level recruitment policy, compare 4 HR-health indicators (working rate, performance, mid-level enthusiasm, mid-level change-count)

Coefficient sensitivity sweeps on the two experience-tuned constants (unsuccessful-promotion attenuation 0.90-0.97; churn-growth coefficient 1.02-1.13) to show outputs are robust to subjective choices

Multilayer formalism done properly with the correct vocabulary (adjacency tensor, supra-adjacency via tensor flattening) cited to Kivela et al. 2014, and scoped down to 14 nodes citing the O(n^(2(d+1))) edge blow-up as justification
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins on three things. First, MODELING FIDELITY TO THE PROMPT: nearly every qualitative clue in the problem (85% fill, mid-level can't be vacant, churn diffuses to neighbors, training matters, CEO:worker salary ratio, hiring delays) is given a concrete, named mechanism rather than hand-waved — judges reward a model that visibly 'answers the question asked.' Second, the PAM + position-as-node abstraction is a genuinely clever, reusable scaffold that makes an otherwise vague HR problem computable and lets one data structure carry the whole simulation; it reads as an original idea, not a textbook template. Third, it DELIVERS DECISION-USEFUL ANSWERS with numbers a supervisor could act on: two-year recruitment/training budgets (26.22σ and 140.15σ), explicit working-rate trajectories at 18/25/35% churn (steady-state ~80/75/67%), and a clear policy recommendation on the no-external-recruitment question backed by 4 indicators and a control-variate design. Add credible engineering hygiene — Monte Carlo with 10-run averaging, a quantified stochastic-error bound, two-coefficient sensitivity sweeps showing robustness — and a non-trivial, correctly-vocabularied multilayer extension (adjacency tensor / supra-adjacency), and you have a paper that is creative, complete, and self-critical. It tackled all parts of a two-part interdisciplinary prompt, which many teams fail to do."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-strong and judge-friendly: a clean Summary that states all three numeric results up front; a Section 1 with explicit 'Our tasks' restatement, literature review, and a Terminology block (defining churn rate with its formula) so notation never surprises the reader; a 'model overview and concepts definition' that defines node/edge/factor/performance/time-unit before any math. The model build is staged didactically — static network first, then the three evolution algorithms, then simulation results — each algorithm paired with its own labeled flowchart (Figures 4, 5). Assumptions are stated locally per algorithm rather than dumped in one list, tying each assumption to the specific mechanism it licenses. Results sections consistently follow a 'Parameter identification -> Model results -> Rationality Analysis' rhythm, and that recurring 'Rationality Analysis' (sanity-checking each result against real HR practice and citing the team-science reference) is a standout habit that signals scientific maturity. Heavy, well-captioned use of figures (working-rate curves, cost scatter, comparative two-policy line charts) makes the quantitative story skimmable. Closes with honest Strengths/Weaknesses. Main blemish: noticeable ESL grammar/typos throughout ('demission', 'osculating', 'gragh') — tolerated by judges because the modeling and figures carry the communication."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many core coefficients (mu, xi1=xi2=1/3, epsilon%=2%, attenuation 0.95, churn-growth 1.02) are 'set by experience' with no data grounding; the model's quantitative outputs (the σ budgets, steady-state rates) are only as trustworthy as these guesses — sensitivity sweeps mitigate but don't validate them

Outputs are in abstract units of σ (one churn-rate's worth?) and basic-productivity units, never tied to real currency or headcount, so the headline budget numbers (26.22σ, 140.15σ) are not externally interpretable

The promotion rule keys off WORKING-TIME/tenure as the ranking criterion and a productivity threshold, but enthusiasm/ability quantification is partly circular (enthusiasm feeds performance, churn feeds enthusiasm) and never independently calibrated

The multilayer section is under-powered relative to its billing: it analyzes only a 14-person sub-office (justified by O(n^(2(d+1))) cost) and never actually couples the multilayer dynamics back into the churn simulation — it stays a descriptive metrics table (Table 1), so Part 2 of the problem (connect HC network to other layers) is answered structurally but not operationally
Friendship and competition layers are asserted with assumed/synthetic edges, not derived from any data, so Table 1's metrics are illustrative rather than evidential

By own admission, retirement, temporary leave, and inter-division operational effects are omitted; the model treats churn probability as uniform across levels in the baseline despite the problem stressing mid-level is different

Some result figures appear idealized/monotone (e.g., perfectly linear cost ramps, suspiciously smooth performance scatter) suggesting schematic rather than raw simulation plots — a judge might probe whether the curves are real output
Pervasive language errors and a few garbled equation renderings reduce precision and could cost clarity points
reusable_patterns: The 'attribute matrix that travels with the agent' pattern (Post Archives Matrix): keep network topology fixed, push all dynamics into a per-node state matrix that re-initializes on turnover — reusable for any staffing/inventory/slot-filling problem
Position/slot-as-node instead of person-as-node when the structure is fixed but occupants change — turns an unbounded people problem into a bounded-graph problem

Three-priority coupled state-machine for entity flow (exit -> internal-fill -> external-fill) with a hard 'critical slots never empty' invariant — a template for any churn/replacement or supply-chain backfill model

Monte Carlo per-entity Bernoulli draw calibrated to a known aggregate rate + N-run averaging + an explicit stochastic-error metric to bound noise — a clean, defensible way to handle randomness and pre-empt 'is this just noise?' questions

Control-variate scenario design: to evaluate a policy change, freeze every parameter and flip only the policy switch, then compare a fixed panel of 3-4 outcome indicators — directly answers 'what if we change X' sub-questions judges love

Encoding each qualitative problem clue as one explicit quantitative update rule, and listing the assumption that licenses it right next to the mechanism — maximizes the 'we addressed the prompt' signal

The recurring 'Parameter identification -> Model results -> Rationality Analysis' subsection template, where every numeric result is sanity-checked against real-world expectation and a cited reference — cheap to apply and strongly boosts credibility
Decompose a tangled real relationship into independent abstracted networks (here via a 'superposition' analogy) then recombine — a justification pattern for simplifying multi-relationship graphs

When a full multilayer/combinatorial model is intractable, scope to a representative sub-unit and cite the complexity bound (O(n^(2(d+1)))) as explicit justification — shows judgment rather than giving up
Use distance-based normalized intensity alpha = (d_max - d_min_path)/d_max to turn graph shortest-paths into a 0-1 relationship-strength weight
